title: Queen vs Advanced Pawn
summary: The famous race — usually the queen wins

steps.title: The Zig-Zag Technique
steps.text:
## Q vs P on the 7th — the famous race

A queen versus a pawn one step from promotion is the most cinematic endgame. The defender's king escorts a pawn that *will queen on the next move* unless the queen can wrestle a tempo. The technique that converts is the **zig-zag** — a sequence of checks and pins that forces the enemy king to **block its own pawn**, giving the queen time to bring her king closer.

### What this endgame looks like

- **King + queen** versus **king + pawn on the 7th rank**.
- The defender's king sits **next to or in front of** the pawn.
- The attacker's king is usually far away.

### The key concept: force the king onto the queening square

Each **check** forces the defending king to move. The trick is to alternate two ideas:

- A **diagonal check** along the queen's natural lines.
- A **pin or block** that costs the defender a tempo to deal with.

When the defender finally has only one legal move — **stepping onto the queening square** — the queen has *one full free move* to bring the king one square closer.

### Procedure to convert

1. **Check** the enemy king with the queen.
2. After the king moves, **pin the pawn** or **threaten promotion-square mate**.
3. The king is forced to **block the pawn**.
4. The attacker's **king takes a step** while the queen rests.
5. **Repeat** until the king arrives. Then mop up.

### Common mistakes

- **Stalemating** the lone king — always check that he has at least one square or is in check.
- Forgetting to **bring up your own king** between manoeuvres — the queen alone cannot win without escort.
- **Capturing the pawn** prematurely — sometimes correct, but sometimes leaves a drawn K vs K.

### A model line (schematic)

{{Qd5+}} Kc1 {{Qd2}} (pinning) Kb1 — and now White takes a tempo with {{Kc4}}, then resumes checks.

> The queen does not need to capture — she only needs tempi.

steps.title: The Exceptions
steps.text:
## Two pawns the queen cannot beat

The zig-zag wins **almost always**. The two famous exceptions are pawns whose queening square is too close to a corner for the defender to be forced onto it:

### 1. Bishop pawn (c/f) on the 7th

The defender uses a hidden **stalemate trick**: when the attacker's queen tries to force the king onto the queening square (c1 or f1), the defending king instead steps to the **corner** (a1 or h1). With the c-pawn or f-pawn frozen, this is **stalemate** — and a draw.

- *Picture it*: White Q somewhere, Black Kb1 with c2 pawn. White plays Qb2+? Black takes — wait, White must avoid stalemate motifs. With perfect defence, the queen cannot force the king off the corner without giving up the pawn.

### 2. Rook pawn (a/h) on the 7th

The **same stalemate trick** works on the corner files. The defending king parks in the corner; the queen can never deliver a pin without stalemating.

### Pawns the queen always beats

- **Knight pawn** (b/g) — no stalemate refuge.
- **Centre pawns** (d/e) — the king has too much room and gets caught.

### How to USE this in your games

1. Before trading down to a queen-vs-pawn race, **identify the file**. If it is a c/f or a/h pawn, **the race is drawn** — find another plan.
2. As the **defender**, push toward a **bishop or rook pawn** whenever you can; even down a queen, you may hold.
3. As the **attacker**, get your **king into the action** during the middlegame so the race is unnecessary.

### What signs tell you you are in this ending

- The position is heading toward queen versus a single passed pawn.
- The pawn is on the **6th or 7th rank** with a king right behind it.
- All other material is gone or about to be exchanged.

### Common mistakes

- Assuming **all pawns lose to a queen** — c, f, a, and h-pawns on the 7th draw with best play.
- Capturing the c- or a-pawn into a **drawn K vs K** without checking the king-distance rule.
- Allowing the defending king to reach the corner unchallenged.

> Queens beat pawns — except the corner four.
